ops.title: go-git refs walk races real git ref writes — retry empty refs, skip .lock names, tolerate readdir-vs-rename misses
ops.body: gitcmd owns ref writes (real `git update-ref` with user credentials), so go-git's dotgit refs walk in this package races real git's write protocol: git creates `<ref>.lock` empty, fills it, then renames it over the ref. Three windows follow. (1) Empty-file window: the walk reads the empty lock and the whole iteration aborts with dotgit.ErrEmptyRefFile — every refs-iteration call site must go through retryEmptyRef (bounded, 10 attempts x 1ms), which outlasts any healthy writer; once exhausted the error surfaces, because a persistently empty file under refs/ means a crashed writer, not a race. (2) Filled-lock window: the walk surfaces a phantom ref named `<ref>.lock` — listings must skip the suffix (git forbids `.lock` in refnames and skips these files in its own iteration). (3) Readdir-vs-rename: enumerating a directory during a same-directory rename may transiently see neither name (unspecified by POSIX), so a listing can miss a ref that exists — accepted as benign; callers re-list, and tests assert presence-conditionally. Any new go-git refs-iteration call site (References, per-ref Reference lookups, glob walks) silently reintroduces (1) and (2) unless routed through retryEmptyRef plus the `.lock` skip. Separately, WriteOpsCommit is deterministic on purpose: entity id = create-commit SHA, so the canonical pack marshal, tree shape, and commit envelope are storage format — the golden SHA/byte tests pin them, and a failing golden test is an id-migration event, never a test to update. Object reads have their own staleness story (pack-index snapshot — see the retry/lookupCommit note).
